text: You shall not press down upon the brow of labor this crown of thorns, you shall not crucify mankind upon a cross of gold.
speech: Cross of Gold Speech (Democratic National Convention)
context: Climax of the speech that made Bryan the Democratic nominee for president at age 36.

text: We say to you that you have made the definition of a business man too limited in its application.
context: Broadening the definition of who counts as a 'business man' in America.

text: There are two ideas of government. There are those who believe that, if you will only legislate to make the well-to-do prosperous, their prosperity will leak through on those below.
context: Critiquing trickle-down economics in favor of direct support for farmers and laborers.

text: The great cities rest upon our broad and fertile prairies. Burn down your cities and leave our farms, and your cities will spring up again as if by magic; but destroy our farms and the grass will grow in the streets of every city in the country.
context: Arguing for the foundational importance of agricultural producers.

text: We do not come as aggressors. Our war is not a war of conquest; we are fighting in the defense of our homes, our families, and posterity.
context: Framing the free-silver movement as defensive rather than revolutionary.

text: Having behind us the producing masses of this nation and the world, supported by the commercial interests, the laboring interests, and the toilers everywhere, we will answer their demand for a gold standard by saying to them: You shall not press down upon the brow of labor this crown of thorns.
context: The full famous peroration.

text: If they dare to come out in the open field and defend the gold standard as a good thing, we will fight them to the uttermost.
context: Challenging the financial establishment directly.

text: The humblest citizen in all the land, when clad in the armor of a righteous cause, is stronger than all the hosts of error.
context: Moral confidence in the righteousness of the populist cause.

text: We believe that the right to coin and issue money is a function of government, and that all of the money needed to transact the business of the country should be issued directly by the government.
context: Core monetary policy argument of the 1896 campaign.

text: I tell you that the great cities of this country are the storm centers of corruption, and that the great cities are the breeding places of the discontent that is now threatening the peace of society.
context: Linking urban poverty and rural distress.

text: This is not a contest between persons. The humblest citizen in all the land, when clad in the armor of a righteous cause, is stronger than all the hosts of error.
context: Emphasizing principle over personality.

text: We have petitioned, and our petitions have been scorned; we have entreated, and our entreaties have been disregarded; we have begged, and they have mocked when our calamity came.
context: Catalog of ignored grievances of farmers and workers.

text: It is the issue of 1776 over again. Our ancestors, when but three millions in number, had the courage to declare their political independence of every other nation; shall we, their descendants, when we have grown to seventy millions, declare that we are less independent than our forefathers?
context: Framing the monetary debate as a new declaration of independence.

text: If they say bimetallism is good, but that we cannot have it until other nations help us, we reply that, instead of having a gold standard because England has, we will restore bimetallism, and then let England have bimetallism because the United States has it.
context: Rejecting subservience to British financial policy.

text: We do not come here as a part of a political machine; we come here as citizens of the United States, and we come here with the determination to do what is best for the people of this country.
context: Populist claim to speak for the broader public interest.

text: The people of Nebraska are for free silver and I am for free silver. I will look to the welfare of Nebraska and I will look to the welfare of the nation.
context: Tying personal conviction to the interests of his state and country.

text: We have been patient and long-suffering. We have waited in the hope that the party of our fathers would come back to the principles upon which it was founded.
context: Expressing disillusionment with the established Democratic leadership.

text: This crime of the ages is the greatest of all crimes, and the man who is responsible for it is the greatest of all criminals.
context: Referring to the demonetization of silver as a historic wrong.

text: We beg no longer; we entreat no more; we petition no more. We defy them!
context: The rhetorical pivot from petition to defiance.

text: Having behind us the commercial interests and the laboring interests and all the toiling masses, we shall answer their demands for a gold standard by saying to them, you shall not press down upon the brow of labor this crown of thorns — you shall not crucify mankind upon a cross of gold.
context: The complete, powerful closing sentence.
